properties.Name: ROM (The Crystal)
properties.Author: Daniel Libeskind
properties.About: A deconstructivist explosion of glass and aluminum that shards through the museum's original stone walls.
properties.Interesting Fact: The design was famously first sketched by Libeskind on a napkin while dining at the ROM.
properties.Designer Note: A dramatic study in 'structural tension'; the way the aluminum and glass 'shards' intersect the original 1914 stone facade is a masterclass in architectural contrast.

properties.Name: Two Bloor West
properties.Author: BDP Quadrangle
properties.About: A high-performance modernization of a prominent Yonge and Bloor intersection corner featuring vertical glass fins.
properties.Interesting Fact: The renovation added a 'shimmer' to the intersection using high-performance glass fins that change appearance based on the viewer's angle.
properties.Designer Note: A study in 'Adaptive Re-skinning.' The project replaced a dated facade with a curtain wall that adds a dynamic, contemporary layer to the Mink Mile.
properties.Clue / Hint: The shimmering glass corner at Yonge and Bloor.

properties.Name: TD Centre
properties.Author: Ludwig Mies van der Rohe
properties.About: The global 'Gold Standard' of the International Style; a cluster of six black-painted steel and glass towers.
properties.Interesting Fact: Mies designed specific window blinds for the entire complex that only have three positions—open, closed, or halfway—to maintain a uniform facade.
properties.Designer Note: 'Less is More' in physical form. The external I-beam mullions guide the eye upward, serving as a masterpiece of geometric precision and material honesty.
properties.Clue / Hint: Look for the black steel towers with the cows in the grass nearby.

properties.Name: TMU SLC
properties.Author: Snøhetta / Zeidler
properties.About: A prismatic, glass-skinned building that serves as a digital-era gateway and student gathering hub on Yonge.
properties.Interesting Fact: The building has no 'back'; all sides are covered in a complex glass skin with a digital frit pattern to control light.
properties.Designer Note: The faceted blue ceiling in the lobby mimics a 'gathering space under a bridge,' creating a vibrant urban entry point.

properties.Name: Mackenzie House
properties.Author: Unknown
properties.About: The final home of William Lyon Mackenzie, Toronto’s first mayor. A rare surviving mid-19th-century Georgian row house.
properties.Interesting Fact: Known as one of Toronto's most haunted sites; the printing press in the basement is said to click on its own at night.
properties.Designer Note: A study in Architectural Persistence. The flat Georgian brickwork stands in sharp contrast to the high-tech glass of the TMU campus surrounding it.
properties.Clue / Hint: The historic brick house tucked behind the modern campus.

properties.Name: Old City Hall
properties.Author: E.J. Lennox
properties.About: A Romanesque Revival masterpiece featuring a massive clock tower and detailed sandstone carvings.
properties.Interesting Fact: The architect secretly carved his own name into the stone eaves after being denied an official plaque.
properties.Designer Note: A masterclass in late 19th-century civic pride. The weight of the sandstone and the intricate carvings project the permanence of city authority.
properties.Clue / Hint: The clock tower at Queen and Bay.

properties.Name: Hockey Hall of Fame
properties.Author: Frank Darling / S. George Curry
properties.About: Housed in a former Bank of Montreal branch, this ornate building is a pinnacle of late Victorian banking architecture.
properties.Interesting Fact: The building contains one of the most beautiful stained-glass domes in Canada, located in the Esso Great Hall.
properties.Designer Note: The Corinthian columns and decorative stone pediments are designed to inspire awe and confidence—the architectural language of wealth and security.
properties.Clue / Hint: Look for the ornate bank building at Yonge and Front.

properties.Name: Brookfield Place (Allen Lambert Galleria)
properties.Author: Santiago Calatrava
properties.About: A six-story high glass and steel pedestrian atrium known as the 'Crystal Cathedral of Commerce.'
properties.Interesting Fact: The design is meant to mimic the canopy of a forest, with white steel columns branching out into an intricate web of glass.
properties.Designer Note: Note how the structure manages to feel organic despite its industrial materials. It is a 'Trace' of nature inserted into the Financial District.
properties.Clue / Hint: The white forest of steel in the Financial District.

properties.Name: Railway Museum (John Street Roundhouse)
properties.Author: Canadian Pacific Railway
properties.About: An outdoor industrial gallery featuring the John Street Roundhouse, the primary repair hub for the Canadian Pacific Railway.
properties.Interesting Fact: The 'Roundhouse' was a functioning repair hub for steam locomotives, featuring a rotating turntable to move massive trains.
properties.Designer Note: A mechanical 'Trace' of the steam era. Walking through the trains allows the reader to physically read the industrial history of Toronto.
properties.Clue / Hint: Look for the steam engines across from the CN Tower.

properties.Name: Gallanough Block
properties.Author: Unknown
properties.About: A moody, mid-century rooftop bar that offers the city's most iconic view of the skyline and university.
properties.Interesting Fact: It is one of the few surviving continuous commercial "blocks" from the early 1900s that still maintains its original rhythmic window bays.
properties.Designer Note: The repetitive "grid" of the windows and the heavy cornice provide a perfect example of balanced industrial-era proportions.

properties.Name: Paupers Pub
properties.Author: Hynes, Feldman & Watson
properties.About: Originally built in 1911 as a branch for the Home Bank of Canada, this Edwardian Classical building features a striking red-brick facade.
properties.Interesting Fact: The 'snake symbol' is a Caduceus—a staff of Hermes, the patron of merchants, carved into the stone to represent prosperity and trade.
properties.Designer Note: Institutional branding via stone. Massive columns and a heavy entablature project immovable security in early 20th-century banking.
properties.Clue / Hint: Look for the staff of the merchants carved in stone above the patio.

properties.Name: Brookfield Place
properties.Author: Santiago Calatrava
properties.About: A soaring, skeletal "forest" of white steel arches that turns a transit corridor into a modern cathedral.
properties.Interesting Fact: The soaring "Allen Lambert Galleria" is often called a "cathedral of commerce" due to its tree-like support columns.
properties.Designer Note: Calatrava’s "biomorphic" ribs turn a functional transit corridor into a rhythmic, organic forest of white steel.

properties.Name: CIBC Square
properties.Author: WilkinsonEyre
properties.About: A high-tech diamond-faceted skyscraper featuring an elevated park suspended over the city's rail lines.
properties.Interesting Fact: It features a one-acre "Park in the Sky" suspended four storeys above the rail corridor.
properties.Designer Note: The "diamond-patterned" facade isn't just for looks; the facets catch the light at different angles to break up the massive scale of the tower.

properties.Name: Robarts Library
properties.Author: Warner, Burns, Toan & Lunde
properties.About: A peak example of Brutalist architecture, designed as a massive concrete "fortress of knowledge" in the shape of a peacock.
properties.Interesting Fact: Often called "Fort Book," it was designed to look like a peacock (or a turkey) from an aerial view.
properties.Designer Note: Brutalism at its peak; the raw, "beton brut" concrete shows the texture of the wooden molds it was poured into—pure material honesty.

properties.Name: OCAD (Sharp Centre)
properties.Author: Will Alsop
properties.About: A playful, pixelated black-and-white "tabletop" that floats on multi-colored pencils high above the street.
properties.Interesting Fact: The "tabletop" structure sits on 12 multi-colored legs, some of which are painted black to "disappear" at night.
properties.Designer Note: A "courageous" architectural UX; it preserves the view of the park by lifting the entire building 26 meters into the air on "pencils."

properties.Name: Massey Hall
properties.Author: Sidney Badgley (Original) / KPMB Architects (Renovation)
properties.About: Toronto’s 'Old Lady of Shuter Street,' a late-Victorian concert hall recently restored to integrate a high-tech glass 'passerelle' bridge system.
properties.Interesting Fact: The famous red doors weren't originally red; they were painted in the 1900s to make the building stand out in the city's industrial smog.
properties.Designer Note: A masterclass in 'Acoustic Logic.' The floating glass bridges allow the original masonry to breathe while providing a modern, transparent 'skin' for movement.
properties.Clue / Hint: Look for the floating glass bridges hugging the old red brick.

properties.Name: St. Lawrence Market
properties.Author: John William Siddall
properties.About: A massive 19th-century public market built directly over the footprint of Toronto’s 1845 City Hall—a building within a building.
properties.Interesting Fact: The South Market building was originally the city's second city hall before being fully converted into a market space in 1902.
properties.Designer Note: A study in 'Structural Layering.' The lower levels still reveal the stone foundations of the original city hall, while the steel trusses represent Victorian-era engineering peaks.
properties.Clue / Hint: Find the massive brick archways where the city's first council used to meet.
